title: Photons, Wavelength, and Energy: The Quantized Ripple

scenes.title: Light as a Wave: $c=f\lambda$
scenes.description: Start with the classical wave picture: stretching wavelength lowers frequency when the wave speed is fixed.
scenes.markdown:
# Light as a Wave

Classical electromagnetism treats light as a wave moving at speed $c$ in vacuum. The basic kinematic link is

$$c=f\lambda.$$

If $c$ is fixed, wavelength and frequency must trade off:

$$f=\frac{c}{\lambda}.$$

A longer wavelength means fewer crests pass a point each second. This part is continuous: in free space, a photon can have any wavelength allowed by the source and propagation conditions.

## What this wave is — and what it is not

The sine wave shown here represents the **electric field** component of a classical electromagnetic wave: $E_y(x,t)=A\sin(kx-\omega t)$, described by Maxwell's equations.

A real EM wave has **two fields** oscillating in phase, perpendicular to each other and to the propagation direction:

- **Electric field** $\vec{E}$: shown here as the sine wave in the $y$-direction.
- **Magnetic field** $\vec{B}$: oscillates in the $z$-direction (perpendicular to the screen), with magnitude $B=E/c$.

We show only the electric field because it is the component that does most of the work on charged matter — it drives electron oscillations, photoelectric ejection, and detector responses. The magnetic component is always present but $c$ times smaller in SI units.

This wave does not depict a single photon. In quantum terms it corresponds to a **coherent state** — a huge number of photons all sharing the same frequency, direction, and phase, like a laser beam or a radio signal. The amplitude $A$ is the peak electric field strength. A brighter beam has a larger $A$, which means more photons per second, not more energetic photons.

A single photon is one quantum excitation of the field mode. It has definite energy $E=hf$ and momentum $p=h/\lambda$, but it does not have a definite classical electric field value. To depict a single photon, you would show a localized **wave packet** — a few oscillations that taper off — not an infinite plane wave.

The quantum content of this scene is the relation $E=hf$: the wave's energy is not continuous but comes in discrete packets, each carrying energy proportional to frequency. The wave picture gives wavelength and frequency; quantization gives the energy rule.

References: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum and https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photon_energy
scenes.prompt: Teach the wave kinematics first. Keep the distinction clear: c = f lambda is a wave relation, while E = hf is the quantum energy relation introduced next. Emphasize that stretching wavelength lowers frequency for fixed c.

steps.title: A travelling EM wave
steps.description: A sinusoidal electromagnetic wave gives the first mental model: a spatially spread disturbance with a wavelength $\lambda$.
steps.prompt: Introduce wavelength as crest-to-crest spacing, amplitude as peak height, and frequency as crests passing per second (period T = 1/f). The wave now visibly travels. Do not talk about photons as tiny balls.

info.content:
**Anatomy of a wave**

- $\lambda$ — crest-to-crest spacing (gold span)
- $A$ — peak height above rest (coral span)
- $f$ — crests passing a point per second; period $T = 1/f$

$$c=f\lambda$$

Relative frequency $\propto 1/\lambda = {{toFixed(1/lambda, 2)}}$

steps.title: Predict: double the wavelength
steps.description: Before revealing the graph: with $c$ fixed, if you double the wavelength $\lambda$, what happens to the frequency $f$? Halves, doubles, or stays the same? And what shape will the $f$ vs $\lambda$ graph be — a straight line or a curve?
steps.prompt: Ask the learner to commit to an answer before advancing. If they hesitate, point back at c = f·lambda: the product must stay fixed. Do not reveal the answer; the next step shows the hyperbola.

info.content:
**Predict first**

Double $\lambda$ (drag the slider from 2 to 4).

1. Does $f$ halve, double, or stay the same?
2. Straight line or curve?

Commit to an answer, then advance to check.

steps.title: Frequency falls as wavelength grows
steps.description: Answer: $f$ halves — $f = c/\lambda$ is an inverse proportion, so the graph is a hyperbola, not a line. With $c$ fixed, the frequency scales like $1/\lambda$. The scale bars below compare $\lambda$ to nanoscale objects: visible light wavelengths are on the order of viruses and bacteria.
steps.prompt: Use the slider to compare short and long wavelengths. Ask the learner what happens to f when lambda doubles. Draw attention to the scale bars: when lambda is around 1, the wavelength is about the size of a flu virus; at lambda 5, it matches the width of an E. coli bacterium. Visible light lives in this extraordinary nanoscale world.

info.content:
**Kinematic link**

Stretch the wave: fewer peaks pass per second.

$$f=\frac{c}{\lambda}$$

steps.title: Visible light: real wavelengths
steps.description: Map the abstract $\lambda$ to real wavelengths. The vertical spectrum bar shows how frequency maps to color. Note: the wave's amplitude represents the peak electric field strength, not its energy or wavelength—amplitude depends on intensity (how many photons), not on color.
steps.prompt: Show how the abstract wavelength maps to visible light. The vertical color bar next to the f-axis shows the direct mapping: higher frequency means shorter wavelength and bluer color. Emphasize that our eyes only see a tiny slice of the full EM spectrum. Point out that the amplitude of the wave is the electric field strength and is independent of wavelength—it depends on the light's intensity, not its color. A dim blue beam and a dim red beam have the same small amplitude; a bright red beam has a large amplitude.

info.content:
**Visible spectrum**

$$\lambda_{real}=(300+80\lambda)\;\text{nm}$$

$$f=\frac{c}{\lambda_{real}}=\frac{3\times10^{8}}{\lambda_{real}}\;\text{Hz}$$

Violet: $380\,\text{nm}$, $789\,\text{THz}$

Red: $700\,\text{nm}$, $428\,\text{THz}$

**Physical constants**

$c=3\times10^{8}\;\text{m/s}$

$h=6.626\times10^{-34}\;\text{J}\!\cdot\!\text{s}$

**Wavelength → frequency**

$$f=\frac{c}{\lambda}$$

| $\lambda$ (nm) | $f$ (THz) | Color |
|---|---|---|
| 380 | 789 | violet |
| 480 | 625 | cyan |
| 550 | 545 | green |
| 600 | 500 | orange |
| 700 | 428 | red |

steps.title: The full picture: $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$
steps.description: An electromagnetic wave has two perpendicular oscillating fields. Until now we showed only the electric field $\vec{E}$ (in $y$). The magnetic field $\vec{B}$ oscillates in $z$, perpendicular to both $\vec{E}$ and the propagation direction. Switch to the Iso view to see both components in 3D. This wave depicts a steady many-photon beam, not a single photon.
steps.prompt: Reveal the magnetic field component oscillating in the z-direction. Explain that every EM wave has both E and B perpendicular to each other and to the propagation direction. We showed only E because it dominates interactions with charged matter. Note B = E/c in SI units; we exaggerate B visually for clarity. Make the beam-vs-photon distinction explicit: this travelling wave is a steady many-photon beam (a coherent state, like a laser), not a single photon — a single photon would be a localized wave packet, shown later in the wave-packet scene. Point out the two arrows at the sample point x0: they are the instantaneous field values — what a detector parked there feels — while the curves show the whole wave at once. If asked about the right-hand rule: E and B flip sign together, so the Poynting vector E cross B is proportional to sine squared — it pulses at twice the wave frequency but always points along propagation, never backward. Define the Poynting vector at first mention: S = (1/mu0) E cross B, the field's energy flux in watts per square meter; its time-average is the beam intensity.

info.content:
**Complete EM wave**

$$\vec{E}=E_0\sin(kx-\omega t)\,\hat{y}$$

$$\vec{B}=\frac{E_0}{c}\sin(kx-\omega t)\,\hat{z}$$

$\vec{E}\perp\vec{B}\perp\hat{x}$

Previous steps showed only $\vec{E}$ because it dominates interactions with charged matter.

**Where this is headed**

Maxwell's wave picture is perfect — until you ask how much energy a hot box should radiate. That question breaks classical physics. Next scene: the crisis.

**What this wave shows**

A steady classical beam — countless photons marching in lockstep (a *coherent state*, like a laser). It is **not** a single photon.

A single photon would be a localized **wave packet** — a few oscillations that taper off. That picture comes later, in the wave-packet scene.

**Instantaneous field values**

Fix a point $x_0$ (gray line): the two arrows show the field values *right now* — $E_y(x_0,t)$ oscillating in $y$, $B_z(x_0,t)$ in $z$, in phase, with $B = E/c$ in SI units (drawn same-height here for visibility).

The curves show the whole wave; the arrows show what a detector parked at $x_0$ actually feels.

**The gold arrow — Poynting vector $\vec{S}$:** the field's energy flow, $\vec{S} = \tfrac{1}{\mu_0}\vec{E}\times\vec{B}$ — power per area (W/m²); its time-average is the beam's intensity.

**Right-hand rule:** $\vec{E}\times\vec{B}$ looks like it should flip as the fields undulate — but $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$ reverse sign *together*, so $\vec{S}\propto\sin^2$ always points **forward** (gold arrow): energy flow pulses at twice the wave frequency but never reverses.

proof.title: From $x=vt$ to $c=f\lambda$
proof.goal: Derive the wave relation $c=f\lambda$ starting from the basic kinematic equation $x=vt$.
proof.prompt: Start from x=vt which every learner knows. Apply it to one wave cycle to get c=f*lambda. Emphasize this is the same formula, not a new law.

steps.label: Basic kinematics
steps.justification: The fundamental kinematic relation for constant speed.
steps.explanation: Distance equals speed times time — the starting point for all wave relations.
steps.prompt: Start from the most basic formula the learner already knows.

nodes.description: Expresses the equality between distance and the product of speed and time.
Distance, the total length traveled.
Speed, the rate of change of position.

enrichment.reasoning: The context describes a fundamental kinematic relation, so the domain is classical mechanics. The symbols are identified as distance and speed.

steps.label: One period of a wave
steps.justification: In one period $T$, the wave advances exactly one wavelength $\lambda$. Set $x=\lambda$, $v=c$, $t=T$.
steps.explanation: A crest travels one full wavelength in one period, so $\lambda = c \cdot T$.
steps.prompt: Connect x=vt to the wave: x becomes lambda, v becomes c, t becomes T.

nodes.description: This relation states that the wavelength is the product of the wave speed and its period.
Wavelength, $\lambda$, the distance over which a wave's shape repeats.
The speed of light, $c$, a universal physical constant.

enrichment.reasoning: The context is about light waves, so the domain is optics. The symbols c and lambda are identified as the speed of light and wavelength.

steps.label: Solve for frequency
steps.justification: Frequency is the reciprocal of period: $f=1/T$. Substitute $T=\lambda/c$.
steps.explanation: Rearranging gives the wave relation: frequency equals speed divided by wavelength.
steps.prompt: Show the algebra step by step: T = lambda/c, so f = 1/T = c/lambda.

nodes.description: Frequency, the number of wave cycles that pass a point per unit of time.
The reciprocal of the period $T$, which defines the frequency of the wave.
Period, the time required for one full cycle of the wave.
Wave speed divided by wavelength, which determines the wave's frequency.
The speed of light in a vacuum, a fundamental physical constant.
The reciprocal of the wavelength, representing the number of waves per unit distance (wavenumber).
Wavelength, the spatial period of the wave over which its shape repeats.
An equation relating frequency $f$ to the period $T$ and to the ratio of wave speed $c$ to wavelength $\lambda$.

enrichment.reasoning: The context discusses light waves, frequency, and wavelength, so the domain is optics. The symbol `c` is identified as the speed of light.

steps.label: The wave equation
steps.justification: Multiply both sides of $f=c/\lambda$ by $\lambda$.
steps.explanation: This is just $x=vt$ applied to one cycle: speed equals frequency times wavelength.
steps.prompt: Emphasize that c = f lambda is not a new law — it is x = v t for one wave cycle.

nodes.description: An equation stating that the speed of a wave is equal to its frequency times its wavelength.
The speed of light in a vacuum, a fundamental physical constant.
The product of frequency and wavelength, which yields the propagation speed of the wave.
The frequency of the wave, representing the number of oscillations per unit time.
The wavelength of the wave, representing the spatial period of the wave.

enrichment.reasoning: The context discusses light, photons, wavelength, and frequency, so the domain is optics. `c` is the speed of light, `f` is frequency, and `λ` is wavelength.

proof.title: Maxwell to $\vec{E}=E_0\sin(kx-\omega t)\,\hat{y}$
proof.goal: (Optional deep dive — requires vector calculus.) Derive the electromagnetic wave equation from Maxwell's equations and show that a sinusoidal $\vec{E}$ field is a solution.
proof.prompt: This derivation uses vector calculus (curl, partial derivatives). Tell novice learners it is safe to skip — the take-away is that Maxwell's equations predict waves moving at exactly c. Start from Maxwell's curl equations in free space. Take the curl of Faraday's law and use the vector identity to get the wave equation. Show the sinusoidal solution and extract the dispersion relation omega = ck.

steps.label: The electromagnetic wave equation
steps.justification: Define $c^2=1/(\mu_0\epsilon_0)$ and rearrange. This is the standard wave equation.
steps.explanation: Maxwell's equations predict that $\vec{E}$ satisfies a wave equation with speed $c=1/\sqrt{\mu_0\epsilon_0}\approx 3\times 10^8$ m/s — the speed of light.
steps.prompt: This is the historic result: Maxwell's equations predict electromagnetic waves travelling at the speed of light. Light IS an electromagnetic wave.

steps.label: Dispersion relation
steps.justification: Substituting the ansatz: $\partial^2 E_y/\partial x^2=-k^2 E_y$ and $\partial^2 E_y/\partial t^2=-\omega^2 E_y$. The wave equation gives $k^2=\omega^2/c^2$.
steps.explanation: Plugging the sine into the wave equation forces $\omega=ck$ — the frequency and wave number are locked together by the speed of light.
steps.prompt: Compute the second derivatives and substitute. The sine cancels, leaving the algebraic constraint omega = ck.

scenes.title: The Ultraviolet Catastrophe
scenes.description: Classical wave theory predicts a hot oven should radiate infinite energy. The fix — energy exchanged in quanta E=hf — is the birth of quantum mechanics.
scenes.markdown:
# The Ultraviolet Catastrophe

## The historical pressure

At the turn of the twentieth century, classical blackbody theory led to the ultraviolet catastrophe: it predicted runaway short-wavelength emission. Planck's resolution was to make exchange of energy discrete, in units proportional to frequency. That idea became the first step toward quantum mechanics.

This scene builds the crisis and its resolution visually: the classical Rayleigh–Jeans spectrum diverges at high frequency, while Planck's quantized spectrum stays finite and matches what hot objects actually emit.
scenes.prompt: Guide the learner through the blackbody crisis: hot things glow, classical theory says every mode gets kT so total energy diverges, Planck's E=hf freezes out expensive quanta. Define 'mode' and 'kT' in plain language before using them.

steps.title: Hot things glow
steps.description: Before any theory, the everyday fact: heat something and it glows. Embers glow dull red, the Sun burns yellow-white, the hottest stars shine blue-white. Predict: heat a piece of iron hotter and hotter — does its glow shift toward red or toward blue?
steps.prompt: Anchor the phenomenon before any theory. Ask the learner to commit: hotter iron glows redder or bluer? Use the everyday anchors — embers about 800 K are dull red, the Sun about 5800 K is yellow-white, the hottest stars are blue-white. Do not mention modes or kT yet. The temperature slider here is in relative units. The gold curve is the measured blackbody spectrum — treat it as an experimental fact here; the theory fight over explaining it comes next.

add.text: the glow of a hot object

info.content:
**Predict first**

Heat a piece of iron hotter and hotter. Does its glow shift toward **red** or toward **blue**?

Drag $T$ along the strip and commit to an answer.

Everyday anchors:

- embers ($\sim$800 K): dull red
- the Sun ($\sim$5800 K): yellow-white
- the hottest stars ($\sim$20\,000 K): blue-white

Current $T = {{toFixed(Temp, 2)}}$ (relative units). This whole scene explains *why* the color shifts.

**The measured spectrum**

Point a spectrometer at any hot object and you get this curve: energy per frequency, with a single peak. Drag $T$ — the peak slides toward higher frequency (bluer glow) exactly as the strip above shows.

Every hot body makes this same shape. *Explaining it* is the crisis ahead.

steps.title: Inside a hot box: modes and $kT$
steps.description: Physicists model a glowing object as a hot box (cavity) full of light waves. Two words before the theory: a *mode* is one allowed standing-wave pattern of light in the box, and $kT$ is the typical thermal energy per jiggle at temperature $T$. Classical physics gives every mode the same average energy $kT$ — and short wavelengths pack in unlimited modes. Predict the total radiated energy of a warm oven.
steps.prompt: Define the jargon before using it: a mode is one allowed standing-wave pattern in the box (point at the three shapes), and kT is the typical thermal energy per jiggle, with k the Boltzmann constant. Then set up the predict question: classical physics gives every mode kT and there are unlimited short-wavelength modes, so what is the classical total? Also explain how to read the chart axes in plain words: frequency across, energy content at each frequency up. Do not reveal the divergence yet.

info.content:
**Two words you need**

- a **mode** = one allowed standing-wave pattern of light in the box (the three shapes shown)
- $kT$ = the typical thermal energy per jiggle at temperature $T$ ($k$ = Boltzmann's constant)

**The classical rule:** every mode gets the same average energy $kT$ — and short wavelengths pack in **unlimited** modes.

**Predict:** what total radiated energy does classical physics assign a warm oven?

*Reading the chart:* frequency increases to the right; the curve height is the energy content at each frequency.

steps.title: The catastrophe, revealed
steps.description: The classical answer is infinity. The red Rayleigh–Jeans curve $u \propto T f^2$ climbs without limit — it shoots off the top of the chart. Real ovens follow the gold Planck curve instead: it agrees with the classical curve at low frequency, then turns over and dies. The red shading is energy the classical rule predicts that simply is not there.
steps.prompt: Reveal the answer to the predict question: classical physics says a warm oven radiates infinite energy, which is absurd — that absurdity is the ultraviolet catastrophe. Walk the two curves: red Rayleigh-Jeans grows like T f squared forever and exits the top of the chart; gold Planck agrees at low frequency then turns over. The red shaded gap is energy that is not actually there. Let the learner drag T (or watch it bounce) and notice the gold peak grows and slides right while red always diverges.

info.content:
**Answer: infinity.**

Classical (red): $u_{RJ} \propto T f^2$ — every mode gets $kT$, and modes never run out, so the curve climbs off the chart. The red shading is energy the classical rule predicts that **is not there**.

Real ovens follow the gold curve — Planck's law:

$$u(f) = \frac{8\pi h f^3}{c^3}\,\frac{1}{e^{hf/kT}-1}$$

Drag $T$: at low $f$ the two curves agree; at high $f$ the gold curve is frozen out.

steps.title: Count the energy
steps.description: Add up the energy frequency by frequency: slide the cutoff $f_{cut}$ to the right. The classical total (red area) grows like $f_{cut}^3$ — without bound. The quantum total (gold area) converges to a finite number proportional to $T^4$: the Stefan–Boltzmann law.
steps.prompt: This step makes the divergence quantitative. As f_cut sweeps right, read the two live totals: the classical area grows like f_cut cubed forever, while the Planck area converges to about 5.5 T to the fourth — the Stefan-Boltzmann law. Emphasize: same box, same temperature, one rule gives infinity and the other gives a finite T-to-the-fourth total that matches experiment.

info.content:
**Add it up, frequency by frequency**

Energy counted up to $f_{cut} = {{toFixed(fcut, 1)}}$:

- classical (red area): $\propto {{toFixed(0.2833*Temp*fcut^3, 1)}}$ — grows like $f_{cut}^3$, **without bound**
- Planck total (gold area): converges to $\propto {{toFixed(5.52*Temp^4, 1)}}$ — **finite**

$$\int_0^{\infty} u(f)\,df = \frac{8\pi^5 k^4}{15 c^3 h^3}\,T^4$$

A finite total, growing as $T^4$ — the Stefan–Boltzmann law, exactly what hot objects do.

steps.title: Planck's move: $E = hf$
steps.description: Planck's resolution: energy exchange is not arbitrary. A mode of frequency $f$ can only trade energy in whole chunks of size $E = hf$. High-frequency chunks cost more than the thermal budget $kT$ can pay, so they are frozen out — the spectrum turns over. Drag $T$: the peak slides right as $f_{peak} \propto T$, and the glow shifts red, to white, to blue — exactly the strip from the first step.
steps.prompt: Land the resolution. Planck quantized the exchange of energy: E = hf per chunk, so high-frequency modes are frozen out because one chunk costs more than kT. Connect the Wien peak marker to the color strip from step 0: as T rises the peak crosses the visible band and the glow shifts red to white to blue. Close with the bridge: this E = hf is the same energy rule the rest of the lesson builds on — it is the bridge from the wave picture to photons. Avoid overclaiming: Planck quantized energy exchange, not spatial wavelength.

info.content:
**Planck's move**

Energy exchange is not arbitrary — a mode of frequency $f$ trades energy only in whole chunks:

$$E = hf$$

High-$f$ chunks cost more than $kT$ can pay, so they are frozen out — the gold curve turns over instead of diverging.

Peak frequency: $f_{peak} = 2.82\,T = {{toFixed(2.82*Temp, 2)}}$ — it slides right as $T$ grows (Wien's law), sweeping the glow from red through white toward blue.

This is the bridge from the wave picture to photons.

proof.title: Taming the catastrophe
proof.goal: Show that classical equipartition makes the blackbody energy diverge, and that Planck's quantization $E_n = nhf$ replaces it with the finite law $u(f) = \frac{8\pi h f^3}{c^3}\,\frac{1}{e^{hf/kT}-1}$.
proof.prompt: Walk the historical derivation at intro-university depth. The two givens (mode density and equipartition) are stated, not derived — say so honestly. The pivotal step is Planck's quantized average: energy comes in chunks of hf, Boltzmann-weighted, and the geometric sums give hf over (e^(hf/kT) - 1). Emphasize the two limits: low frequency recovers the classical kT, high frequency is exponentially frozen out. That freeze-out is what makes the total integral finite.

steps.explanation: How many modes live near frequency $f$? The number grows like $f^2$ — short wavelengths pack in ever more standing-wave patterns. This is the 'unlimited modes' fact from the visualization.
steps.prompt: Explain that this counts the standing-wave patterns per unit volume near frequency f. The key qualitative point: the count grows like f squared, so there is no shortage of high-frequency modes.

steps.label: Classical equipartition

kt.label: Thermal energy per mode (k = Boltzmann constant)

steps.justification: Classical statistical mechanics: in thermal equilibrium every quadratic degree of freedom carries the same average energy, so every mode gets $kT$ regardless of its frequency.
steps.explanation: The classical rule is democratic: every mode — whatever its frequency — holds the same average energy $kT$. Democracy plus unlimited modes is where the trouble starts.
steps.prompt: Define kT in plain words: the typical thermal energy per jiggle at temperature T. Stress that the classical rule pays every mode the same, no matter how high its frequency.

steps.label: Rayleigh–Jeans law
steps.justification: Energy density = (number of modes at $f$) × (average energy per mode).
steps.explanation: Multiply the two givens: the classical spectrum grows like $f^2$ forever. This is the red curve shooting off the top of the chart.
steps.prompt: Point at the red curve in the scene: this formula is that curve. Modes times energy per mode. Ask what happens as f keeps growing.

steps.label: The catastrophe
steps.justification: Integrating the Rayleigh--Jeans density $u_{RJ}(f)=8\pi f^2 kT/c^3$ over all frequencies diverges: $\int_0^{F} f^2\,df = F^3/3 \to \infty$ as $F\to\infty$. The mode count keeps growing with $f$, so the total energy is unbounded --- the ultraviolet catastrophe.
steps.explanation: Add up $kT$ per mode over unlimited modes and the total energy of a warm oven comes out infinite. Every oven should vaporize the universe in ultraviolet light. Something in the assumptions is wrong.
steps.prompt: Tie this to the count-the-energy step: the red area grows like f_cut cubed and never stops. Name it: this absurdity is the ultraviolet catastrophe, and it means one of the two givens must be wrong.

nodes.description: The relation showing that the total energy density, calculated by integrating the Rayleigh-Jeans law over all frequencies, diverges to infinity.
The lower bound of the integral, representing zero frequency.

nodes.label: infinity
nodes.description: The divergent total energy predicted by classical physics for a blackbody radiator, known as the ultraviolet catastrophe.
The integral of the Rayleigh-Jeans energy density $u_{RJ}(f)$ over all frequencies, representing the total radiated energy per unit volume.
The differential element for frequency $f$, used in the integration.
The Rayleigh-Jeans law $u_{RJ}(f)$, which gives the spectral energy density of a blackbody as a function of frequency $f$.
The frequency of electromagnetic radiation.

steps.label: Planck's quantization

denom.label: Boltzmann-weighted cost of a quantum vs the thermal budget

steps.justification: Allow a mode only the energies $0, hf, 2hf, \dots$, weighted by Boltzmann factors $e^{-nhf/kT}$. The two geometric series in the average $\sum n h f e^{-nhf/kT} / \sum e^{-nhf/kT}$ sum to $hf/(e^{hf/kT}-1)$.
steps.explanation: Planck's move: a mode cannot hold an arbitrary sliver of energy — only whole chunks of $hf$. Averaging over Boltzmann-weighted chunk counts replaces the flat $kT$ with an average that *depends on frequency*.
steps.prompt: This is the pivotal step. Energy comes in chunks of hf; the thermal average over whole chunks gives hf over (e^(hf/kT) minus 1). If the learner wants the algebra, sketch the two geometric series; otherwise stress the meaning: expensive chunks are rarely afforded.

steps.label: Planck's law

denom.label: The factor that tames the catastrophe

steps.justification: Same mode count $g(f)$ as before — only the average energy per mode changed.
steps.explanation: Multiply the unchanged mode density by the new quantized average. This is the gold curve: it rises like $f^3$ at first, then the denominator takes over and kills it.
steps.prompt: Point at the gold curve: same mode counting, new energy rule. Note that only the second given was replaced — the mode density survives intact.

steps.explanation: When chunks are cheap, quantization is invisible and the classical $kT$ reappears — that is why the red and gold curves agree at low frequency. When one chunk costs more than the thermal budget, the mode can afford nothing at all: frozen out.
steps.prompt: Connect both limits to the chart: at low f the curves hug each other (classical physics recovered); at high f the gold curve dies exponentially. Freeze-out is the physical answer to why the glow is not ultraviolet.

steps.label: The total is finite
steps.justification: With the substitution $x = hf/kT$ the integral becomes $\int_0^\infty x^3/(e^x - 1)\,dx = \pi^4/15$, a finite number.
steps.explanation: Quantizing energy exchange — $E = hf$ — is exactly what makes the total finite: hot objects radiate a finite power growing as $T^4$, matching experiment. This one formula is the birth of quantum mechanics, and the rest of the lesson runs on it.
steps.prompt: Close the arc: E = hf turned infinity into T to the fourth. Preview the next scene: quantization explains blackbody glow, but the direct experimental proof that light itself arrives in chunks is the photoelectric effect.

scenes.title: The Photoelectric Effect: Light Arrives in Chunks
scenes.description: Einstein's 1905 explanation of photoelectric emission — Kmax = hf − φ — is the experimental proof that light delivers energy one quantum at a time.
scenes.markdown:
# The Photoelectric Effect

Shine light on a metal and electrons pop out — but only if the light's *frequency* is high enough. Below the threshold, no intensity is ever enough; above it, even dim light ejects electrons instantly. A classical wave, whose energy accumulates smoothly, predicts exactly the opposite. Einstein's 1905 resolution: light arrives in chunks of energy $E = hf$, and one electron absorbs one chunk, all or nothing:

$$K_{max} = hf - \varphi.$$

Millikan spent a decade trying to refute this equation and ended up confirming it — and measuring Planck's constant $h$ from the slope.
scenes.prompt: Walk the learner through the photoelectric effect as the experimental proof of photons. Define the electron-volt and the work function in plain language at first use. Emphasize the all-or-nothing character: intensity sets the count of electrons, frequency sets each electron's maximum energy.

steps.title: Predict: can bright red light do it?
steps.description: A dim red wave washes over a metal plate. Classically, wave energy accumulates: absorb long enough and any electron should eventually gather enough to escape. Predict: must bright red light eventually eject electrons? Does dim blue light stand any chance?
steps.prompt: Set up the classical prediction and make the learner commit. A classical wave delivers energy continuously, so brightness times time should eventually free electrons at any color — that is the classical bet. Also define the new units plainly at first use: one electron-volt is the energy an electron gains crossing one volt, about 1.6e-19 joules, and a visible photon carries 2 to 3 eV; the work function phi is the escape fee the metal charges an electron; remind them K equals one half m v squared. Do not reveal the experimental answer yet.

info.content:
**Predict first**

Classically, a wave's energy **accumulates**: shine long enough and any electron should eventually gather enough to escape.

1. Must bright **red** light eventually eject electrons?
2. Does dim **blue** light stand any chance?

Commit to both answers, then advance.

**New units, plainly**

- $1\;\text{eV}$ = the energy an electron gains crossing 1 volt $\approx 1.6\times 10^{-19}$ J. A visible-light photon carries $\sim$2–3 eV.
- $\varphi$ (work function) = the **escape fee** the metal charges an electron.
- Kinetic energy: $K = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$.

steps.title: All or nothing
steps.description: The experiment says no. Light arrives as photons — chunks of energy $E = hf$ — and one electron absorbs one photon, all or nothing. Raise the intensity $I$: more photons arrive, each carrying the same energy. Raise the frequency $f$ past the work function $\varphi$: electrons suddenly fly out, even at the dimmest setting.
steps.prompt: Reveal the all-or-nothing rule and let the learner play. Intensity adds photons (count the gold dots as I rises) but never changes the energy per photon. The electron appears only when f exceeds phi — at any intensity — and its speed grows like the square root of the surplus f minus phi. Contrast with the classical bet from the predict step: red light below threshold never ejects, no matter how bright or how long. Point at the live yes/no readout as they drag f across phi.

info.content:
**All or nothing**

One photon carries $E = hf$. One electron absorbs **one** photon — or nothing. Energy never accumulates across photons.

Electron ejected? **{{fph > phi ? "yes — instantly" : "no — at ANY intensity"}}**

- Raise $I$: more photons, same energy each.
- Raise $f$ past $\varphi$: electrons fly, even at $I = 1$.

Surplus speed $\propto \sqrt{f - \varphi}$.

steps.title: Millikan's graph: $K_{max}$ vs $f$
steps.description: Plot the maximum kinetic energy of the ejected electrons against the light's frequency. Below the threshold $f_0 = \varphi/h$: a dead zone — no electrons at any intensity. Above it, the data fall on a straight line $K_{max} = hf - \varphi$. The white dot is the current experiment; drag $f$ to move it along the line.
steps.prompt: Introduce the K-max versus f graph as the quantitative face of the experiment Millikan actually ran. Walk the anatomy: dead zone left of the threshold f0 = phi/h where nothing comes out, then a straight line rising from the threshold. The operating point mirrors the apparatus on the left — drag f below phi and the point drops to the axis while the electron vanishes. Read the live K-max value from the info panel; note the axes are in chart units, with K in eV in spirit.

info.content:
**Millikan's plot**

Above threshold the data fall on a straight line:

$$K_{max} = hf - \varphi$$

Current experiment: $f = {{toFixed(fph, 1)}}$, $\varphi = {{toFixed(phi, 1)}}$

$K_{max} = {{toFixed(max(0, 0.9*(fph - phi)), 2)}}$ (chart units)

Left of the threshold $f_0 = \varphi/h$: the dead zone — **no** electrons at any intensity, exactly what the classical wave forbids.

steps.title: The slope is $h$
steps.description: Predict: swap the metal for one with a different escape fee $\varphi$ — what moves, the slope or the intercept? Drag $\varphi$ and watch: the line translates sideways, but its slope never changes. Every metal gives the same slope — Planck's constant itself. That is how Millikan measured $h$.
steps.prompt: Predict-then-reveal inside one step. First ask: changing the metal changes phi — does that change the line's slope or just shift it? Then have the learner drag phi against the gray reference line of the first metal: the gold line translates, the slope stays fixed. The slope is h (h over e when plotting stopping voltage) — universal, independent of the metal. Close the arc: Millikan set out to refute Einstein's equation, confirmed it instead, and extracted h from graphs like this one.

info.content:
**Predict, then drag $\varphi$**

Swap the metal — a different escape fee $\varphi$. What moves: the **slope** or the **intercept**?

Drag $\varphi$: the line **translates**; the slope never changes. Every metal gives the same slope

$$\text{slope} = h \quad (\text{as stopping voltage: } h/e)$$

Millikan spent a decade trying to refute this line — and ended up measuring Planck's constant from it.

Intensity sets the electron **count**; frequency sets each electron's **energy**. The classical wave predicted the opposite on both counts.

proof.title: Einstein's photoelectric equation
proof.goal: Derive $K_{max} = hf - \varphi$ from the photon picture, and read off its two testable consequences: a frequency threshold $f_0 = \varphi/h$ and a universal slope $h$.
proof.prompt: Keep the derivation honest about how little it needs: one photon of energy hf, one escape fee phi, one absorption event, and energy bookkeeping. The power is in the consequences — threshold and universal slope — which are exactly what the scene's Millikan graph shows and exactly what a classical wave cannot produce. Define eV and work function plainly if asked.

scenes.title: One Photon Energy vs Light Intensity
scenes.description: The energy of one photon depends on frequency; intensity depends on how many photons arrive.
scenes.markdown:
# Photon Energy

The Planck-Einstein relation is

$$E=hf=\frac{hc}{\lambda}.$$

A shorter-wavelength blue photon has more energy than a longer-wavelength red photon. But that statement is about **one photon**.

A beam's total delivered energy also depends on photon count:

$$E_{\text{total}}=Nhf.$$

So the useful correction is:

- Blue photon: higher energy per photon than red.
- Many red photons: can carry more total energy than one blue photon.
- Intensity: mainly photon number flux.
- Color/frequency: energy per photon.
scenes.prompt: Correct the common misconception directly. Say: one blue photon has more energy than one red photon, but many red photons can carry more total energy. Separate energy per photon from photon count/intensity.

steps.title: Energy per photon
steps.description: A red photon ($\lambda=700$ nm) carries $1.77$ eV; a blue photon ($\lambda=450$ nm) carries $2.76$ eV. The blue photon has more energy because $E=hf$.
steps.prompt: Emphasize that energy per photon is set by frequency. Use the eV numbers on the bars to ground the comparison.

info.content:
$$E_B>E_R$$
when $f_B>f_R$.

This compares **one photon to one photon**.

steps.title: Photon count changes total energy
steps.description: Use the red photon count slider. Many lower-energy photons can exceed the energy of one higher-energy photon.
steps.prompt: Let the learner move N_red. Emphasize that intensity is count per area per time, while frequency sets energy per photon.

info.content:
**Correct comparison**

A blue photon has more energy than a red photon.

But $N$ red photons carry

$$E_{total}=Nhf_R.$$

For large enough $N$, that total can exceed one blue photon.

steps.title: Color is not brightness
steps.description: Frequency controls energy per photon. Photon number controls intensity. The two knobs are different.
steps.prompt: Finish by separating two axes: frequency/color and intensity/count. Mention that real beams also involve area and time.

| Quantity | Meaning |

| $f$ | energy per photon |
| $N$ | number of photons |
| intensity | photon flux and total power |

scenes.title: When Wavelengths Become Discrete
scenes.description: Free waves can vary continuously; constrained waves must fit boundary conditions.
scenes.markdown:
# Continuous vs Discrete Wavelengths

Free photons in open space can have a continuous range of wavelengths. Discrete spectra appear when a system is constrained.

The simplest analogy is a standing wave in a box of length $L$. If the wave must vanish at both walls, only waves that fit are allowed:

$$L=n\frac{\lambda}{2},\qquad n=1,2,3,\ldots$$

For a nonrelativistic particle in a box, the energy levels scale as

$$E_n=\frac{n^2h^2}{8mL^2}.$$

So a useful correction is: the allowed wavelengths scale like $1/n$, but the particle-in-a-box energies scale like $n^2$, not like $n$.

Atoms are more complicated than a literal box, but the same structural idea survives: boundary conditions and allowed quantum states produce discrete emission lines.
scenes.prompt: Teach quantization as a boundary-condition effect. Make clear that free photons are not forced into a discrete wavelength ladder; confinement or bound-state structure creates discreteness.

steps.title: A free spectrum is continuous
steps.description: Without boundary conditions selecting modes, wavelength can vary continuously.
steps.prompt: Start from the contrast: free space permits a continuum. Bound systems create a ladder.

info.content:
**Free system**

No wall condition forces a specific wavelength ladder.

Wavelength is continuous in the ideal free-space model.

steps.title: Boundary conditions pick standing waves
steps.description: The wave must have nodes at both walls, so only integer half-wavelengths fit: $L=n\lambda/2$.
steps.prompt: Use the n slider to show exactly fitting waves. The walls require zero amplitude at both ends.

info.content:
**Allowed wavelengths**

$$L=n\frac{\lambda}{2}$$

$$\lambda_n=\frac{2L}{n}$$

steps.prompt: Correct the linear-energy misconception. Explain that momentum scales like n, and kinetic energy goes like p^2.

info.content:
**Correction**

Not $E_n=nhc/(2L)$ for a massive particle in a box.

Instead:

$$E_n=\frac{n^2h^2}{8mL^2}$$

steps.prompt: Use this as the bridge from box analogy to atomic spectra. Avoid saying atoms are literal boxes.

info.content:
**Atomic emission**

$$E_{photon}=E_{high}-E_{low}$$

Discrete energy levels create spectral lines.

proof.prompt: Walk from the box boundary condition to quantized energy levels: only half-integer wavelengths fit, de Broglie makes a momentum ladder linear in n, kinetic energy squares it into E_n proportional to n^2.

steps.label: Boundary condition: half-waves fit the box
steps.justification: the starting expression

steps.label: de Broglie wavelength relation
steps.justification: De Broglie's hypothesis states that the momentum $p_n$ of a particle is inversely proportional to its wavelength $\lambda_n$, with Planck's constant $h$ as the proportionality constant.

steps.label: Substitute $\lambda_n$
steps.justification: Substitute the given expression for $\lambda_n$ from the starting equation, $\lambda_{n} = \frac{2 \cdot L}{n}$, into the De Broglie relation.

steps.label: Simplify the momentum
steps.justification: To simplify a complex fraction of the form $\frac{A}{B/C}$, multiply the numerator $A$ by the reciprocal of the denominator $B/C$, resulting in $\frac{A \cdot C}{B}$.

steps.label: Kinetic energy of the particle
steps.justification: The kinetic energy $E_n$ of a particle with momentum $p_n$ and mass $m$ is given by the formula $E_n = \frac{p_n^2}{2 \cdot m}$.

steps.label: Substitute the momentum
steps.justification: Replace $p_n$ in the kinetic energy formula with the derived expression $p_n = \frac{h \cdot n}{2 \cdot L}$.

steps.label: Expand the squared term
steps.justification: Apply the power rule for fractions $\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)^2 = \frac{A^2}{B^2}$ and for products $(A \cdot B)^2 = A^2 \cdot B^2$ to the term $\left(\frac{h \cdot n}{2 \cdot L}\right)^2$.

steps.label: Square the denominator
steps.justification: Calculate the square of the term in the denominator: $(2 \cdot L)^2 = 2^2 \cdot L^2 = 4 \cdot L^2$.

steps.label: Combine the fractions
steps.justification: To simplify a fraction with a nested fraction in the numerator, such as $\frac{A/B}{C}$, multiply the denominator of the main fraction $C$ by the denominator of the nested fraction $B$, yielding $\frac{A}{B \cdot C}$.

steps.label: Simplify — the $n^2$ energy ladder
steps.justification: Perform the multiplication $2 \cdot 4 = 8$ in the denominator to simplify the expression.

scenes.title: Photon as Wave Packet and Detection Event
scenes.description: A photon is a quantum excitation: spread out in possibility, localized in interaction.
scenes.markdown:
# What Is a Photon?

A photon is a quantized excitation of the electromagnetic field. It is not a tiny classical ball and not a classical water wave.

The useful synthesis is a wave packet:

$$\Psi(x,t)=\int A(k)e^{i(kx-\omega t)}\,dk.$$

Before detection, the quantum state can be spatially spread out. Detection is localized and all-or-nothing: a detector either records an interaction or it does not.

A careful wording is:

- Before measurement: spread-out quantum state or probability amplitude.
- At measurement: localized detection event.
- Measurement updates the quantum state description.

This avoids the misleading picture of a physical classical wave instantly collapsing across the universe.
scenes.prompt: Use cautious quantum language. Say the photon is a quantized field excitation, described by a state that can be spatially spread out, with localized detection. Avoid implying a classical physical wave literally collapses.

steps.title: A localized packet is built from many waves
steps.description: A packet is made by superposing many wave numbers $k$. Narrower packets require a broader spread of wavelengths.
steps.prompt: Introduce superposition qualitatively. This is a conceptual visualization, not a full photon field calculation.

info.content:
$$\Psi(x,t)=\int A(k)e^{i(kx-\omega t)}\,dk$$

A packet is spread out, but not infinitely uniform.

steps.title: Detection is localized
steps.description: The spread-out state predicts probabilities. The detector records a localized event.
steps.prompt: Use the detector point to distinguish state description from detection event.

info.content:
**Photon detection**

Spread-out state.

Localized interaction.

All-or-nothing detector click.

steps.title: The final mental model
steps.description: A photon is spread out in possibility and localized in interaction. Wave and particle are incomplete classical metaphors.
steps.prompt: Close the lesson with the core insight. Invite questions about particle in a box, atomic orbitals, or black holes as next topics.

info.content:
**Core insight**

A photon is not a tiny ball flying through space.

It is a quantum entity: spread out in possibility, localized in interaction.

scenes.title: Spreading Is Not Stretching
scenes.description: A wave packet can spread through space while each photon's energy stays fixed; redshift changes energy per photon.
scenes.markdown:
# Spreading vs Stretching

This is the most important distinction in the lesson.

## Spreading

A beam or wave packet expands over a larger area. The same photon energy is distributed across a larger cross-section, so intensity drops. Energy per photon stays the same.

## Stretching

The wavelength itself grows. Since

$$E=\frac{hc}{\lambda},$$

stretching the wavelength lowers the energy per photon.

Redshift is a stretching effect. Doppler redshift comes from relative motion. Gravitational redshift comes from climbing out of a gravitational field. Cosmological redshift is described by

$$\lambda_{obs}=\lambda_{em}(1+z).$$

In expanding spacetime, local conservation laws still hold, but a single global total energy for the universe is not generally well-defined.
scenes.prompt: Make the distinction crisp: spreading changes intensity, stretching changes photon energy. For cosmological redshift, say global total energy is not generally well-defined in GR rather than saying energy disappears.

steps.title: Spreading dims the beam

steps.prompt: Explain inverse-area intuition without over-formalizing it. Energy per photon is unchanged by spreading alone.

info.content:
**Spreading**

Bigger footprint means lower intensity.

Energy per photon remains $E=hf$.

steps.description: Redshift increases wavelength. The wave becomes longer and $E=hc/\lambda$ decreases.
steps.prompt: Use the z slider to show wavelength stretching. Emphasize energy per photon, not photon count.

info.content:
**Stretching**

$$\lambda_{obs}=\lambda_{em}(1+z)$$

$$E_{obs}=\frac{E_{em}}{1+z}$$

Not: energy disappears.

scenes.title: Where Intuition Breaks: Black Holes
scenes.description: Extreme gravity sharpens the lesson: photon energy depends on observer and spacetime, and singularities require unfinished physics.
scenes.markdown:
# Black Holes and the Limits of the Picture

Near a black hole, intuition based on ordinary wave packets becomes unreliable.

Correct statements:

- Falling photons can blueshift for local observers.
- Photon energy contributes to the black hole's mass-energy when absorbed.
- Photons do not become little classical balls compressed to a mathematical point.
- Singularities are not fully described by established quantum theory and general relativity together.

The honest ending is that black holes point toward quantum gravity, which remains unsolved.
scenes.prompt: Use this as a careful epilogue. State what is known without pretending the lesson solves quantum gravity. Correct the idea that photons literally collapse into a point.

steps.title: Falling light blueshifts locally
steps.description: A falling photon can be measured with increasing frequency by local observers deeper in the gravitational field.
steps.prompt: State observer dependence. Do not imply there is one universal photon energy in curved spacetime.

info.content:
**Near strong gravity**

Observed photon frequency depends on observer and spacetime geometry.

Falling photons can blueshift locally.

steps.title: Absorbed energy changes the black hole
steps.description: If the photon is absorbed, its energy contributes to the black hole's mass-energy.
steps.prompt: Make this conservative: absorbed energy contributes to mass-energy. Avoid details of horizon thermodynamics.

info.content:
**Absorption**

Photon energy is not ignored.

Absorbed energy contributes to black-hole mass-energy.

steps.title: Do not overextend the picture
steps.description: Photons do not literally collapse into a classical point. The singularity problem needs quantum gravity.
steps.prompt: Close by acknowledging the unsolved part. Use this as a boundary on the model, not a mystery flourish.

info.content:
**Model boundary**

Not: photons collapse into one physical point.

Better: established physics becomes incomplete at singularities.
